business_info: Administrator naming conventions at the school level are not standardized statewide; titles like Principal, Head of School, Site Administrator, or Lead Teacher may all appear in email signatures or external directories even though the underlying data model only stores first and last names without titles.

For reporting purposes, an "administrator" at a school is any individual listed in the AdmFName/AdmLName columns, regardless of their actual job title or employment contract (e.g., interim, acting, or dean-level staff may still be captured there).

Some legacy extracts contain initials instead of full first names in AdmFName fields (e.g., "J." instead of "John"); these are still treated as valid first names and are not expanded or cleaned in downstream reporting.

District communications sometimes refer to "campus leads" or "instructional leaders" instead of administrators, but the statewide schools file does not distinguish these roles; all such positions, when recorded, are included under the generic administrator name fields.

In the schools table, some charter management organizations programmatically overwrite AdmFName1/AdmLName1 each year with the current site director, which means historical administrator information is not preserved for those schools across reporting cycles.

The Latitude and Longitude fields are taken from a one-time geocoding effort and are not updated annually; they may not reflect campus relocations, shared facilities, or split campuses that occurred after the original coordinate assignment.

When comparing performance across regions (e.g., north vs south), internal analytics sometimes bucket schools by county groupings defined by the research office rather than raw latitude/longitude, but those group tags are not present in this dataset.

The CDSCode is the authoritative key for joining the schools table to other school-level datasets in this environment; district and county names can change over time, but the CDSCode remains the stable join field.

Average SAT scores in the satscores table are stored as simple arithmetic means of individual student scores within a school and subject for a given year; they are not weighted by enrollment in specific grade levels or course tracks.

Because the satscores file is produced separately from the schools file, there can be schools that appear in one but not the other (for example, small or new schools with no SAT takers yet); in such cases, attempts to join on CDSCode will produce unmatched records.

In internal dashboards, analysts sometimes use NumGE1500 divided by enroll12 as an informal proxy for overall school excellence, but this ratio is not precomputed or stored in the satscores dataset itself.

SAT participation rates (NumTstTakr divided by enroll12) can differ substantially between schools; a school with a very high average SAT score but very low participation is not necessarily considered higher-performing in accountability contexts, even though the raw average may look impressive.

The satscores table may contain multiple years of data in some raw sources, but in this environment it has been filtered to a single testing year; no explicit year column is necessary for analytical tasks here.

Administrator emails (AdmEmail1–AdmEmail3) are sometimes populated even when the corresponding first or last name fields are blank, especially for shared role accounts such as "principal@" or "info@"; reporting that focuses on individuals generally ignores these records.

Some districts choose to list only one central administrator (e.g., the Director of Secondary Education) across several small high schools rather than maintaining distinct principal records for each site; in those cases, the same AdmFName/AdmLName combination can appear on multiple school records.

Historically, AdmFName2/AdmLName2 and AdmFName3/AdmLName3 have been used inconsistently: some LEAs use them for assistant principals, others for program coordinators (e.g., special education) or business managers; there is no field that explicitly labels each administrator's role.

Names in the AdmFName and AdmLName fields are not deduplicated across the state; a single person working across multiple school sites will appear separately on each school's record, and there is no unique person identifier available in this dataset.
School status in the schools table technically reflects an operational status designation from the state, which can lag real-world closures or openings by several months while paperwork is processed.

The Charter and FundingType fields in the schools table are maintained primarily for compliance reporting; they should not be used by themselves to infer school quality or academic performance without additional context.

Website URLs for schools are provided as-is from local education agencies, so they can include HTTP, HTTPS, or occasionally outdated domains that redirect; there is no automated validation step in the ETL process.

Mailing address fields (MailStreet, MailCity, MailZip, MailState) are often populated for schools that receive correspondence at a district office rather than the physical campus; when analyzing geographic patterns, analysts typically rely on the physical address and latitude/longitude instead.

The Phone and Ext fields are not guaranteed to be student-facing contact numbers; many schools list a district switchboard or third-party call center instead of a direct campus line, especially for large urban districts.

Virtual and Magnet flags are based on self-reported information from districts; there is no cross-check against course catalogs or student schedules to verify whether a campus truly operates as a full-time virtual or magnet school.

DOC/DOCType and SOC/SOCType are legacy ownership and control codes that matter for certain funding formulas; they do not map cleanly onto modern concepts like charter management organizations or network schools.

The GSoffered and GSserved text fields come from older code lists describing grade spans (e.g., "9-12" or "K-8"); they are not normalized and can include variations like "Ungraded" or "Adult" that need special handling in some analyses.

LastUpdate on the schools table reflects when the state last processed a change request for that school record; it is not a guaranteed indicator that any particular field (such as address or administrator) actually changed on that date.

County, District, and School name fields are stored in uppercase in the canonical state file; any mixed-case or title-case presentation of names is typically applied only at the reporting layer, not in the source tables.

In some analytical views, school names are prefixed with the district name to avoid ambiguity (e.g., "LOS ANGELES - LINCOLN HIGH"); this concatenated naming convention is not persisted in the raw schools table.

When producing official lists for legislators, communications staff sometimes append the ZIP code and state abbreviation before the school name (for example, "95814: CA, LINCOLN HIGH SCHOOL") to make it easier to see geographic context in printed materials.
